revision.text: #REDIRECT [[Shinto_Shrine#Kasuga-zukuri]]
#REDIRECT [[Shinto_shrine#Kasuga-zukuri|Kasuga-zukuri]]

[[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]]{{nihongo|'''Kasuga-zukuri'''|春日造}} is a traditional Japanese architectural style which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''honden''. It is characterized by the extreme smallness of the building composed of four interconnected units, each 1x1 ''ken'' in size.<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, this translates in just 1.9 m x 2.6 m each.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The complex faces south, and the unit further to the east is considered the first.
The roof is, as always in shrines, [[gabled|gable]] ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根}}), decorated with ''[[chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', covered with cypress bark and curved upwards at the eaves. Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''tsumairi'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines having a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are to be found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref>
While seemingly completely different, both styles possess characteristics that show they evolved from temporary shrines built for mountain worship.
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japan-art-stub}} {{arch-style-stub}}
[[ja:春日造]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

[[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]]{{nihongo|'''Kasuga-zukuri'''|春日造}} is a traditional Japanese architectural style which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''honden''. While seemingly completely different, both styles possess characteristics that show they evolved from temporary shrines built for mountain worship.

While seemingly completely different, both styles possess characteristics that show they evolved from temporary shrines built for mountain worship.

Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''tsumairi'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines having a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are to be found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref>

[[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|275 px|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]]{{nihongo|'''Kasuga-zukuri'''|春日造}} is a traditional Japanese architectural style which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''honden''. It is characterized by the extreme smallness of the building composed of four interconnected units, each 1x1 ''ken'' in size.<ref>The ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, this translates in just 1.9 m x 2.6 m each.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The complex faces south, and the unit further to the east is considered the first.
The roof is, as always in shrines, [[gable|gabled]] ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with ''[[chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', covered with cypress bark and curved upwards at the eaves. Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''tsumairi'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines having a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are to be found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref>
==''Nagare-zukuri'' and ''kasuga-zukuri''== While seemingly completely different, the ''nagare-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the second most popular style in Japan, the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'', common around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
They for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that the shrine had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/> They also share a veranda covered by a roof in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japan-art-stub}} {{arch-style-stub}}

[[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|275 px|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]]{{nihongo|'''Kasuga-zukuri'''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''honden''. It is characterized by a building composed of four interconnected units, each extremely small) (1x1 ''ken'' in size).<ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, this translates in just 1.9 m x 2.6 m each.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The complex faces south, and the unit further to the east is considered the first.

It is characterized by a building composed of four interconnected units, each extremely small) (1x1 ''ken'' in size).<ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, this translates in just 1.9 m x 2.6 m each.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The complex faces south, and the unit further to the east is considered the first.
The roof is, as always in shrines, [[gable|gabled]] ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), covered with cypress bark and curved upwards at the eaves. Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''tsumairi'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
They for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also share a veranda covered by a roof in front of the main entrance, which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.

[[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|275 px|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]]{{nihongo|'''Kasuga-zukuri'''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''honden''. It is characterized by a building composed of four interconnected units, each extremely small (1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size).<ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, this translates in just 1.9 m x 2.6 m each.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> The complex faces south, and the unit further to the east is considered the first. Each of the units possesses a veranda covered by an extension of the roof.
The roof is, as always in shrines, [[gable|gabled]] ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), covered with cypress bark and curved upwards at the eaves. Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''tsumairi'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The Kasuga-zukuri is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.<ref name="NKS"/>

(The Kasuga-zukuri is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>

[[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|275 px|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]]{{nihongo|'''Kasuga-zukuri'''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] architectural style which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''. It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''tsumairi'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is, as always in shrines, [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The Kasuga-zukuri is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.<ref name="NKS"/>

[[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|275 px|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of 3 joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]]{{nihongo|'''Kasuga-zukuri'''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''. It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The Kasuga-zukuri is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|Hiraoka Shrine's ''honden'' </gallery> ==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kasuga-Zukuri}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

revision.comment: Robot - Speedily moving category Japanese architecture‎ to [[:Category:Architecture of Japan]] per [[WP:CFDS|CFDS]].

The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.<ref name="NKS"/>

revision.text:
{{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''. It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple gabled roof.<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kasuga-Zukuri}}
[[Category:Shinto architecture]]

{{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''. It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kasuga-Zukuri}}
[[Category:Shinto architecture]]

{{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''. It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kasuga-Zukuri}}
[[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] [[Category:Dd]]

This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>

(The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>

==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

(The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}

The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kasuga-Zukuri}}

While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kasuga-Zukuri}}

While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>

(The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}

While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
