{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kasuga-Zukuri}}

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}

(The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[:en:Gable|Gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] around [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[:en:Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

(The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[:en:Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[Shinto shrine]] [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[:en:Gable|Gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] around [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[:en:Rafter|Rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[:en:Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}}

(The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[:en:Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

(The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[:en:Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[es:Kasuga-zukuri]] [[fr:Kasuga-zukuri]] [[ja:春日造]] [[ru:Касуга-дзукури]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3193781]] Wikidata name: Kasuga-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3193781]] Wikidata name: Kasuga-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[:en:Gable|Gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] around [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[:en:Rafter|Rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[:en:Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>

<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kasuga-zukuri]][[es:Kasuga-zukuri]][[fr:Kasuga-zukuri]][[ja:春日造]][[ru:Касуга-дзукури]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3193781]] Wikidata name: Kasuga-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3193781]] Wikidata name: Kasuga-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[:en:Gable|Gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] around [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[:en:Rafter|Rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[:en:Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kasuga-zukuri]][[es:Kasuga-zukuri]][[fr:Kasuga-zukuri]][[ja:春日造]][[ru:Касуга-дзукури]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3193781]] Wikidata name: Kasuga-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3193781]] Wikidata name: Kasuga-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] which takes its name from [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''[[:en:Tsumairi|Tsumairi]]'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is [[:en:Gable|Gable]]d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) or ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] around [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]].<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal [[:en:Rafter|Rafter]] (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple [[:en:Gable roof|gabled roof]].<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>

(The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kasuga-zukuri]][[es:Kasuga-zukuri]][[fr:Kasuga-zukuri]][[ja:春日造]][[ru:Касуга-дзукури]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kasuga-zukuri]][[es:Kasuga-zukuri]][[fr:Kasuga-zukuri]][[ja:春日造]][[ru:Касуга-дзукури]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kasuga-zukuri]][[es:Kasuga-zukuri]][[fr:Kasuga-zukuri]][[ja:春日造]][[ru:Касуга-дзукури]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kasuga-zukuri]][[es:Kasuga-zukuri]][[fr:Kasuga-zukuri]][[ja:春日造]][[ru:Касуга-дзукури]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3193781]] Wikidata name: Kasuga-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3193781]] Wikidata name: Kasuga-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style}} which takes its name from [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken}}'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi}}'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is {{ill|Gable|en|Gable|WD=Q1161370|lt=Gable}}d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi}}'' (vertical) or ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} around {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal {{ill|Rafter|en|Rafter|WD=Q1154705|lt=Rafter}} (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple {{ill|Gable roof|en|Gable roof|WD=Q149617|lt=gabled roof}}.<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->

While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} around {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal {{ill|Rafter|en|Rafter|WD=Q1154705|lt=Rafter}} (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style}} which takes its name from [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken}}'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi}}'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is {{ill|Gable|en|Gable|WD=Q1161370|lt=Gable}}d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi}}'' (vertical) or ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} around {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal {{ill|Rafter|en|Rafter|WD=Q1154705|lt=Rafter}} (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple {{ill|Gable roof|en|Gable roof|WD=Q149617|lt=gabled roof}}.<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} around {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal {{ill|Rafter|en|Rafter|WD=Q1154705|lt=Rafter}} (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>

{{Short description|Shinto architectural style}} {{italic title}} [[File:Udamikumari Shr Kami detail.jpg|thumb|The ''honden'' at [[Uda Mikumari Shrine]] Kami-gū is made of three joined ''Kasuga-zukuri'' buildings]] {{nihongo|'''''Kasuga-zukuri'''''|春日造}} is a traditional {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style|1=Shinto architecture}} which takes its name from [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]''.
==Description== It is characterized by the use of a building just 1x1 ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken|1=Ken (unit)}}'' in size with the entrance on the gabled end covered by a veranda.<ref name="NKS"/><ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building, as in this case.</ref> In Kasuga Taisha's case, the ''honden'' is just 1.9 m x 2.6 m.<ref name="jaka">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kasugazukuri.htm Kasuga-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Supporting structures are painted vermilion, while the plank walls are white.<ref name="jaka"/> It has a {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi|1=Tsumairi}}'' (also called ''tsumairi-zukuri'')|妻入・妻入造}} structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the gabled side.<ref name="jaka"/>
The roof is {{ill|Gable|en|Gable|WD=Q1161370|lt=Gable|1=Gable}}d ({{nihongo|''kirizuma yane''|切妻屋根|gabled roof}}), decorated with purely ornamental poles called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi|1=Chigi (architecture)}}'' (vertical) or ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi|1=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal), and covered with cypress bark.<ref name="jaka"/>
After the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, this is the most common {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} style. While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} around {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal {{ill|Rafter|en|Rafter|WD=Q1154705|lt=Rafter|1=Rafter}} (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>
==''Kasuga-zukuri'' and ''nagare-zukuri''== [[File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|thumb|left|[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]]'s ''honden'']]While superficially completely different, the ''kasuga-zukuri'' actually shares an ancestry with the most popular style in Japan, the ''nagare-zukuri''.<ref name="NKS">{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese|page=28}}</ref>
The two for example share pillars set over a double-cross-shaped foundation and a roof which extends over the main entrance, covering a veranda. (The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) The foundation's configuration is typical not of permanent, but of temporary shrines, built to be periodically moved. This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
The styles also both have a veranda in front of the main entrance, a detail which makes it likely they both evolved from a simple {{ill|Gable roof|en|Gable roof|WD=Q149617|lt=gabled roof|1=Gable roof}}.<ref name="NKS"/>
==Gallery==
<gallery> File:Hiraoka Shrine honden.jpeg|[[Hiraoka Shrine]]'s ''honden'' </gallery> ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

While the first is common all over Japan, however, shrines with a ''kasuga-zukuri'' ''honden'' are found mostly in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} around {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009</ref> If a diagonal {{ill|Rafter|en|Rafter|WD=Q1154705|lt=Rafter|1=Rafter}} (a {{nihongo|''sumigi''|隅木}}) is added to support the portico, the style is called {{nihongo|''sumigi-iri kasugazukuri''|隅木入春日造}}.<ref name="ko"/>

(The ''Kasuga-zukuri'' is the only ''tsumairi'' style to possess this last feature.) This shows that, for example, both the ''nagare-zukuri'' [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] and Kasuga Taisha used to be dedicated to a mountain cult, and that they had to be moved to follow the movements of the ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="NKS"/>
